prompt: Edo-period Japanese ukiyo-e woodblock print. A kimono-fitting scene in a textile merchant's workshop. In the foreground, one kimono displayed on a wooden stand is rendered with extraordinary, obsessive detail — every thread of its pattern individually inked, every fold of the fabric described with impossibly fine parallel lines, the pattern memorizing the exact contours of one specific body's proportions. Behind it, a row of identical kimonos hang on a rack, each rendered as simple flat outlines filled with unmodulated color — the same garment, plainly cut, fitting no one body in particular. Two women in the background attempt to wear the copies; the garments pull and bunch awkwardly. Flat areas of unmodulated indigo, ochre, muted vermillion, and cream. Hard black keyblock outlines on every form. Visible woodgrain texture in the background. Asymmetric diagonal composition. No photographic shading, no gradients, no cast shadows. Stacked vertical spatial depth, not linear perspective. Fine parallel hatching only in fabric folds. Authentic Edo-period ukiyo-e aesthetic, Utagawa school. No text, no labels, no numbers, no diagrams anywhere in the image.
tradition_approach: Flat unmodulated color within hard keyblock outlines, visible woodgrain texture, asymmetric diagonal composition, limited indigo-ochre-vermillion palette, stacked vertical depth instead of linear perspective — all characteristic of the Utagawa school.
